title: Meat The Veals

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Tobias had been posing as an English nanny named Mrs. Featherbottom
jokes.setup: Michael comes home and sees Tobias, whom he'd already seen earlier that day
jokes.punchline: Tobias had been posing as an English nanny named Mrs. Featherbottom — the entire disguise premise is revealed deadpan by the narrator
jokes.explanation: The Mrs. Featherbottom running gag is a brilliant sustained character joke. Tobias's complete lack of self-awareness paired with a Mrs. Doubtfire pastiche is perfectly on-brand. The narrator's deadpan delivery of something utterly insane as if it's normal is classic AD. High rewatch bonus because it rewards familiarity with Tobias.

jokes.text: Who'd like a banger in the mouth? Oh, right. I forgot. Here in the States you call it a sausage in the mouth.
jokes.setup: Tobias in disguise as Mrs. Featherbottom offers breakfast to the family
jokes.punchline: He 'corrects' the British term 'banger' to 'sausage in the mouth,' which is equally sexual-sounding — the correction makes it worse
jokes.explanation: This is a standout joke of the episode. The structure is almost perfect: Tobias makes a double-entendre, appears to self-correct for cultural reasons, and then delivers an equally (or more) phallic replacement. It's quintessential Tobias — his total obliviousness to the sexual reading of his words is his defining character trait. The 'correction' that makes things worse is a beautiful escalation structure. Highly quotable, perfectly economical.

jokes.text: We just call it a sausage. [Narrator] The family, of course, knew it was Tobias...
jokes.setup: Tobias says Americans call it 'a sausage in the mouth'
jokes.punchline: Michael flatly corrects just that part — 'We just call it a sausage' — ignoring the bigger absurdity of the entire situation, then the narrator confirms the family knows it's Tobias
jokes.explanation: A two-part joke: Michael's flat correction focuses on the wrong element (the word 'sausage' rather than 'in the mouth'), and then the narrator confirms the obvious — the whole family knows it's Tobias but plays along. The layering of absurdity is very AD. Economical and well-timed.

jokes.text: But the house had never been cleaner.
jokes.setup: The family knows Tobias is posing as the nanny but hasn't exposed him
jokes.punchline: The narrator offers the pragmatic, absurdly practical reason they tolerate the ruse: the house is clean
jokes.explanation: Short, sharp, and perfectly timed. The joke works because the punchline reframes the entire absurd situation through an utterly mundane lens. Five words that recontextualize why the family maintains the fiction. Very AD in its economy.

jokes.text: Which means I don't have anything to wear for my premiere. The premier- of Canada.
jokes.setup: Lindsay complains she has nothing to wear for her 'premiere'
jokes.punchline: George Michael interrupts to clarify — 'the premier of Canada' — suggesting Lindsay is dating a Canadian political figure
jokes.explanation: The wordplay between 'premiere' (film event) and 'premier' (political leader) is clever, and the casually absurd revelation that Lindsay is dating a Canadian prime minister is very funny. The beat lands cleanly. Not as tight as the best jokes in the episode but well-constructed.

jokes.text: He's going out with my gym teacher.
jokes.setup: George Michael corrects the 'premier of Canada' line
jokes.punchline: He clarifies Lindsay is actually dating his gym teacher — a much more banal and humiliating reality than a head of state
jokes.explanation: Effective misdirection pay-off from the previous joke. After the premier/premiere wordplay, 'my gym teacher' is a perfect comedic deflation. Brief and efficient.

jokes.text: Yeah. And if I don't get your report on it, I won't be able to pass.
jokes.setup: Maeby, posing as a film executive, asks her subordinate for coverage on 'Voices In America: History In Prespective.' He reveals it's a 9th grade history textbook.
jokes.punchline: Maeby drops all pretense and openly admits she needs the studio employee's report to pass her class
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly layered joke. Maeby is a teenager running a film studio, and she's having a professional employee do her actual homework. The reveal is perfectly structured — the employee's deadpan 'It's a ninth grade history textbook' sets it up, and Maeby's total brazenness in admitting her scheme is the punchline. The joke rewards understanding of her whole con. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: On it. Because I have a feeling it's a piece of- [Bleeps]
jokes.setup: Maeby's employee agrees to write the book report, eager to please
jokes.punchline: He's enthusiastically using studio-speak ('it's a piece of—') to describe what is literally a 9th grade textbook, then gets bleeped
jokes.explanation: A functional joke — the bleep gag works because the studio exec applies entertainment industry cynicism to a 9th grade textbook. Not the strongest joke in the scene but contributes to the rhythm.

jokes.text: Maybe we just need a new- [Bleeps] housekeeper.
jokes.setup: The narrator notes Maeby's job is wreaking havoc with her language
jokes.punchline: Maeby drops a bleep-worthy word into casual home conversation, confirming the language contamination is real
jokes.explanation: The joke is efficient — narrator sets up the idea, then Maeby immediately demonstrates it in a domestic context. The bleep in a family setting is a good button. Works as a callback to the previous bleep.

jokes.text: I'm sure she would have ironed it for you had you told her about it before she changed out of her bloody work clothes. But I am off then.
jokes.setup: Lindsay complains Mrs. Featherbottom didn't iron her blouse
jokes.punchline: Tobias (as Mrs. Featherbottom) defends himself in third person and then exits, completely breaking from the nanny persona in his irritation while maintaining the accent
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: Tobias speaks about 'her' (himself) in third person, which is absurd; he's defending himself against his own wife while in disguise; and 'bloody work clothes' is a perfectly placed Britishism that makes the delusion funnier. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: Having my day ruined with whatever you're about to ask me to do.
jokes.setup: Lucille asks Michael what he's doing tomorrow
jokes.punchline: Michael preemptively answers the real question — he knows whatever she's about to ask will ruin his day, so he skips the pretense
jokes.explanation: A solid character joke. Michael's weary resignation — answering the actual subtext rather than the surface question — is very true to his character and the family dynamic. Clean and efficient.

jokes.text: He is aware that he's not married to you, right?
jokes.setup: Lucille says Oscar wants to throw her a party for her wedding anniversary
jokes.punchline: Michael points out the obvious absurdity — Oscar is celebrating an anniversary with someone else's wife
jokes.explanation: Michael as straight man, pointing out the logical impossibility in a deadpan one-liner. Works efficiently. The joke is in the understated framing of something deeply weird as just slightly irregular.

jokes.text: Not now. It's my time of the month. / Not now. I don't want to risk having a baby. It could kill me.
jokes.setup: Oscar has perceived Lucille pulling away from him, so he tries to approach her
jokes.punchline: Two excuses in rapid succession, escalating in absurdity: first a mundane one, then a medically ridiculous one (she could die from pregnancy — she is clearly too old)
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is excellent — the first excuse is believable, the second is darkly absurd given Lucille's age. The cold cruelty of Lucille's dismissals, combined with the dark joke about her advanced age, is perfectly character-specific. The 'it could kill me' line is a great example of dark comedy through implication.

jokes.text: Well, he didn't return when I lost my hand. But you not only embrace it, but I saw you kissing it while you thought I was sleeping.
jokes.setup: Oscar says he worries George Sr. might show up and Lucille will want him back
jokes.punchline: Buster reveals that Oscar was kissing Buster's hook hand while Buster slept — a deeply disturbing act presented matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: This is a great dark joke. The revelation that Oscar was kissing the hook hand while Buster slept is bizarrely disturbing and perfectly in keeping with the show's willingness to go dark. The deadpan delivery ('I saw you kissing it while you thought I was sleeping') makes it funnier. Strong character integration — only Buster could deliver this.

jokes.text: Well, I'm sorry if I don't want you doing one of your stupid puppet shows.
jokes.setup: Lucille says she wants the whole family at the party
jokes.punchline: Buster's response apparently involved something about a puppet show, which Lucille immediately and harshly shuts down — the dismissal implies Buster has done this before
jokes.explanation: Lucille's cruel dismissiveness toward Buster is a reliable comedic engine. The specificity of 'your stupid puppet shows' suggests a history, which is funny. Works as character comedy even without the full context.

jokes.text: I did that once, Mom. And a lot of people thought it was pretty cool.
jokes.setup: Lucille dismisses puppet shows
jokes.punchline: Gob defensively claims he only did it once and it was 'pretty cool' — but the narrator and subsequent flashback prove otherwise
jokes.explanation: Gob's defensive self-justification is perfectly in character. 'A lot of people thought it was pretty cool' is faint praise that implies the opposite. The setup for the Franklin flashback.

jokes.text: Can I tell you somethin', my man? Sure, Franklin. You are one cool- [Bleeps] / Speakin' of mothers, let me give that oatmeal some brown sugar.
jokes.setup: Flashback to Gob introducing Franklin, his Black puppet, to hip up his act
jokes.punchline: Franklin calls Gob a racial slur (bleeped), then sexualizes Gob's mother with an oatmeal metaphor
jokes.explanation: The Franklin bits walk a tightrope that AD handles cleverly: the joke is on Gob's oblivious racism in creating a character that immediately insults and humiliates him. The 'brown sugar/oatmeal' line is genuinely inventive as a euphemism. The joke is that Gob's attempt to seem hip backfires spectacularly. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Get off my wife, you bastard!
jokes.setup: Franklin makes a sexual remark about Gob's mother
jokes.punchline: Gob responds as if Franklin is a real person making advances on his real wife — breaking the puppet/performer relationship entirely
jokes.explanation: One of the best Franklin jokes. Gob completely forgets he is Franklin — he responds to his own puppet as if it's an autonomous threat to his marriage. The escalation from 'entertaining act' to 'domestic betrayal' is absurd and brilliant. 'You bastard!' directed at his own hand is peak Gob character comedy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Franklin said some things Whitey wasn't ready to hear. / He also said some things that African-Americany wasn't ready to hear either.
jokes.setup: Gob is beaten outside a club in Torrance after his puppet act
jokes.punchline: The narrator says Franklin offended white people; Gob adds Franklin also offended Black people — making the puppet a uniquely equal-opportunity offensive act
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke structure. The narrator sets up the first half (Franklin offended 'Whitey'), and then Gob casually adds that Franklin also offended Black audiences — and then Gob's clumsy coinage 'African-Americany' is its own joke within a joke. The self-undermining nature of Gob's social awareness is perfectly captured. 'African-Americany' is inspired.

jokes.text: Why do you have the baby monitor out? / She wants her man back. / On my son. / Yo, man. You're gonna get your sorry white ass thrown in jail.
jokes.setup: Lucille catches Michael with a baby monitor; he tries to cover by saying he's listening to his son
jokes.punchline: The monitor is actually picking up George Sr. via Franklin the puppet — and Franklin blurts out an incriminating line about jail just as Michael tries to redirect
jokes.explanation: This is a masterful multi-layered joke. Michael is caught, improvises a cover story, but the baby monitor betrays him by broadcasting Franklin threatening someone about jail — which is both absurd (it's a puppet) and dangerously revealing (it implies George Sr. is nearby). The interleaving of four different conversations is classic AD density. Structure is almost perfect.

jokes.text: My advice, get one of those teddy bears with the cameras in them. I had one in Buster's room to make sure he wasn't getting too serious with anyone. As it turned out, he got too serious with the teddy bear.
jokes.setup: Lucille gives Michael surveillance advice about monitoring George Michael's relationship
jokes.punchline: She reveals she spied on Buster with a teddy bear camera, and the punchline is that Buster became sexually attached to the teddy bear
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and one of the best in the series. The structure is perfect: setup (surveillance advice), escalation (she already did this with Buster), punchline (Buster sexualized the bear). Three separate revelations in rapid succession. It works on multiple levels: Lucille's controlling surveillance of her children, the dark implication about Buster's psychology, and the utter casualness with which she delivers what is an incredibly disturbing fact. 'He got too serious with the teddy bear' is an all-time AD line. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Gangy's having an anniversary party? / Seems that way. / Is Franklin gonna be there? / See that, Mike? Kids love Franklin.
jokes.setup: Gob and Michael discuss the anniversary party
jokes.punchline: George Michael asks specifically about Franklin, which Gob immediately takes as vindication that Franklin is beloved by all
jokes.explanation: A tight little joke about Gob's delusional self-regard. He interprets a kid knowing about Franklin as 'kids love Franklin' — typical Gob spinning a neutral fact into massive validation. Works in the rhythm of the scene.

jokes.text: I just don't want him to point out my cracker ass in front of Ann.
jokes.setup: Discussion of whether Franklin will be at the party
jokes.punchline: Gob's concern about Franklin is that the puppet — who he voices and controls — might say something embarrassing about him. He treats Franklin as a fully autonomous threat.
jokes.explanation: The joke here is that Gob sincerely fears what his own puppet might say about him in public. 'My cracker ass' is funny on its own, but the premise — that Gob cannot control his own act — is what makes it a great character joke. Strong originality within the Franklin running gag.

jokes.text: [Narrator] In fact, Lindsay was currently wearing it on her middle toe, roast beef.
jokes.setup: Michael says his father would never part with something so precious as the engagement ring
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Lindsay is wearing it on her middle toe, and adds 'roast beef' — a reference to 'this little piggy,' implying the toe in question is a specific one, making the ring placement an implicit middle finger
jokes.explanation: This is the most technically sophisticated joke in the episode. First layer: the ring is on Lindsay's toe (crass, funny). Second layer: it's on her middle toe (implicit obscene gesture). Third layer: 'roast beef' is the 'this little piggy' nursery rhyme designation for the middle toe — which is actually 'this little piggy had none,' making it a triple-coded joke. The narrator casually adds 'roast beef' as if it's a helpful anatomical clarification, which makes it even funnier. Exceptional craft. High rewatch bonus — most viewers miss the nursery rhyme layer.

jokes.text: I feel like God moved her here from Ohio to be with me. / [George] Her? / Well, that's the first I've heard of this. / Well, then who said 'Her?' / Me. That was — That was me. / But, um, her?
jokes.setup: George Michael tells Michael he's thinking of getting pre-engaged to Ann
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s disembodied 'Her?' via the baby monitor, which Michael desperately tries to cover, while also himself saying 'her?' — the running 'Her?' gag about Ann's plainness
jokes.explanation: The 'Her?' running gag is one of AD's most beloved. This particular iteration is exceptional because it's George Sr. saying it via hidden baby monitor, Michael desperately covering for it, and then Michael himself also saying 'Her?' — three layers of the same joke in ten seconds. The chaos of Michael trying to attribute the voice to himself while also expressing his own doubt is perfectly constructed.

jokes.text: I had it once. A man I loved — / [Tobias enters] Here's young Maeby's blouse. / And then things took a turn somehow.
jokes.setup: Michael says young love is exciting and mentions he had it once — implying a woman
jokes.punchline: Tobias enters mid-sentence (as Mrs. Featherbottom), and Michael's trailing '...things took a turn somehow' becomes a callback to his marriage to Lindsay/Tobias's gender ambiguity
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed joke. Michael starts to describe young love with 'A man I loved,' which already sounds off. Tobias enters in drag. Michael trails off with 'things took a turn somehow' — which is devastatingly accurate about his own marriage and also about Tobias himself. The double meaning only lands on rewatch when you realize how much is packed in. Strong rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Master Bluth. I haven't seen you in a week's time. / Actually, you tried to put a banger in my mouth this morning.
jokes.setup: Tobias (as Mrs. Featherbottom) greets George Michael as if they've never met
jokes.punchline: George Michael matter-of-factly reminds 'Mrs. Featherbottom' that she tried to put a banger in his mouth that morning — recalling joke #2 with perfect deadpan
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'banger in the mouth' is perfectly executed. George Michael saying it with complete composure — as if this is a normal thing to remind someone of — is the joke. The phrase landing again, now from the recipient's perspective, is a great structural callback.

jokes.text: Hey, Dad. Can you drive me to Ann's? / N-N-N- / Y-Yes. / Great. I'll wait in the car.
jokes.setup: Michael has just resolved to tell George Michael he's against the relationship, but realizes if he says no he'll drive George Michael toward Ann
jokes.punchline: Michael's internal conflict manifests as a stuttered 'N-N-N-' that becomes 'Yes' — he can't even say no
jokes.explanation: A great structural joke. The setup is Michael deciding his strategy, and the punchline is his immediate failure to implement it. The stutter ('N-N-N-') perfectly conveys his internal war, and 'Y-Yes' is a beautifully defeated capitulation. Very economical.

jokes.text: [George] Get your loser hand out of my ass. / Hello, Son. / Oh, Dad. I thought- Dad? / This is where you've been hi- Michael.
jokes.setup: Gob goes to check on 'the one friend who didn't mind having him around' — implying a person
jokes.punchline: Gob has been visiting his father hiding in the model home, and George Sr. is yelling at Franklin (thinking it's a real hand), then realizes it's Gob, then Michael walks in
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered reveal: 'the one friend who didn't mind having him around' turns out to be his fugitive father. 'Get your loser hand out of my ass' implies Franklin has gone somewhere inappropriate, playing with the puppet-as-autonomous-being joke. Then the cascade of people being surprised works beautifully. Strong rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Oh, are you a churchgoing man? / I am. Sometimes. Yes. I don't go religiously. / I mean, I'm not a nut about it. / Because there is no such thing as a nut when it comes to religion, Pastor.
jokes.setup: Michael meets Pastor Veal and is asked if he's a churchgoing man
jokes.punchline: Michael says he doesn't go 'religiously' (meaning obsessively), then panics and says he's 'not a nut about it' — and then doubles down, assuring the pastor there's no such thing as a religious 'nut'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The wordplay on 'religiously' is excellent — Michael uses the idiom ('I don't go religiously') and immediately realizes it sounds like an insult to religion. He then tries to fix it with 'I'm not a nut about it,' which is worse. Then, to dig himself even deeper, he confidently tells a pastor that religious nuts don't exist. The escalating self-sabotage is beautifully structured and very true to Michael's character. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I've met a few. [re: religious nuts]
jokes.setup: Michael says 'there is no such thing as a nut when it comes to religion'
jokes.punchline: Pastor Veal quietly and calmly responds 'I've met a few' — a perfect deadpan correction
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum impact. The deadpan correction from a pastor is exactly the right response and earns a big laugh. Perfect economy score.

jokes.text: This is your wife. Didn't — Didn't realize you could remarry. / What? / Is this Ann's mother? / I'm Mrs. Veal. / I'm sorry. I just — I did not expect somebody so young. / Next you're gonna say how Ann and I can be sisters. / No, I'd never say that.
jokes.setup: Michael meets Mrs. Veal and is shocked by how young and attractive she is compared to Ann
jokes.punchline: Michael says he didn't expect someone 'so young,' Mrs. Veal sets up the cliché compliment ('Next you'll say we could be sisters'), and Michael immediately refuses to say it — the anti-compliment that is actually a brutal insult to Ann
jokes.explanation: Superb misdirection structure. Mrs. Veal sets up the obvious compliment ('you'll say we could be sisters'), which is a social script everyone knows. Michael's 'No, I'd never say that' is the anti-punchline — he won't give the expected compliment because it would imply Ann is as attractive as her mother, which Michael clearly doesn't believe. The refusal is simultaneously more honest and more devastating than the cliché would have been. High rewatch value.

jokes.text: Oh, my gosh. / Oh, my gosh. / Well, that is something to celebrate. Can we open up some sparkling apple cider? / No. / Oh, did you want us to go down to the liquor store and get you some liquor?
jokes.setup: Michael tells the Veals about George Michael's plan to pre-engage, hoping they'll disapprove
jokes.punchline: The devout religious family is delighted by the news and celebrates with apple cider; when Michael says no, they assume he wants liquor — revealing their ignorance of secular adult concerns
jokes.explanation: A good reversal joke — Michael expected disapproval, got enthusiasm. The apple cider gag is a nice touch (devout abstainers). The 'liquor store' suggestion is funny because the Veals think Michael's 'no' means he wants something stronger, which is both naïve and oddly welcoming for conservative Christians.

jokes.text: Who can blame you? You gotta lock that down.
jokes.setup: The Veals describe getting serious at a young age
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Veal uses surprisingly pragmatic, almost cynical dating language ('lock that down') in the context of a devout religious relationship
jokes.explanation: The incongruity of a pastor's wife using 'lock that down' in a deeply sincere, religious context is the joke. Also foreshadows Mrs. Veal's later secular temptations. Works on first viewing and gains a rewatch layer.

jokes.text: [Narrator] And that's when Michael devised a new plan to win the Veals' disapproval.
jokes.setup: The Veals are enthusiastic about the pre-engagement
jokes.punchline: The narrator announces Michael's new plan with the same portentous tone used for legitimate plans, but the plan is the deeply petty goal of making a religious family dislike his son's girlfriend
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan announcement of Michael's scheme — 'to win the Veals' disapproval' — is a neat irony joke. The show often uses the narrator to deflate the dignity of its characters' plans. Functional and efficient.

jokes.text: Hey, Gob. Mom wants Franklin at the party. / Yeah. What? / No, no, no. I don't want to talk — Hey, Franklin.
jokes.setup: Michael calls Gob and asks for Franklin to come to the party (as part of his plan to scare off the Veals)
jokes.punchline: Gob's indignant 'I don't want to talk—' immediately collapses into his Franklin persona ('Hey, Franklin'), suggesting he can't even maintain a conversation without the puppet
jokes.explanation: Gob's inability to have a human conversation without transitioning into Franklin is a great character beat. The rapid collapse from resistance to puppet engagement confirms that Franklin is his primary mode of relating to the world.

jokes.text: Sorry. Just stuffing the mushroom. / You realize that's Tobias, don't you? / If he's gonna raid my closet, he's gonna work for it.
jokes.setup: Lucille asks where the food is while setting up for the party
jokes.punchline: Tobias is 'stuffing the mushroom' (as Mrs. Featherbottom), Buster helpfully points out it's Tobias, and Lucille reveals she's fully aware and is extracting labor from him in exchange for closet access
jokes.explanation: Excellent Lucille character joke. The reveal that she's been aware all along and is effectively enslaving Tobias in exchange for clothing access is perfectly Lucille — amoral, transactional, and completely unsentimental. 'If he's gonna raid my closet, he's gonna work for it' is a great line.

jokes.text: [Tobias attempts an entrance to enchant his daughter] [Singing In Falsetto] / Ohhhh! / We shan't be telling your mother this, shan't we?
jokes.setup: Tobias (as Mrs. Featherbottom) attempts an enchanting entrance to impress Maeby
jokes.punchline: The entrance goes catastrophically wrong (he injures his ankle), and 'Mrs. Featherbottom' desperately swears Maeby to secrecy in broken nanny-speak
jokes.explanation: A strong physical comedy moment. The slapstick of Tobias injuring himself during a bid to impress his own daughter, while maintaining the nanny persona, and then the conspiratorial 'We shan't be telling your mother this, shan't we?' — addressing the wrong person (his wife) through the wrong persona — is densely layered absurdism. The double 'shan't' is a great touch.

jokes.text: Tobias falls attempting a dramatic entrance for Maeby while singing in falsetto, injures his ankle
jokes.setup: Narrator reveals Tobias attempted 'an entrance that he hoped would enchant his daughter' — in full Mrs. Featherbottom costume
jokes.punchline: The entrance ends in injury — Tobias hurts his ankle trying to make a musical, dramatic entry in a nanny costume
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy bit that plays on Tobias's desperate need to impress through performance. The injury underscores that his theatrical ambitions consistently exceed his physical abilities. Solid visual gag.

jokes.text: Ann just called. They had a predawn mass, and then they're gonna go to mass, so — / Well, Ann's got a great deal of mass.
jokes.setup: George Michael explains Ann will be late because of church services
jokes.punchline: Lucille takes 'mass' (church service) literally and applies it to Ann's physical mass — a fat joke disguised as religious terminology
jokes.explanation: One of the great one-liners in the episode. The double meaning of 'mass' (Catholic liturgy vs. physical mass/weight) is perfectly exploited. Lucille's cruelty is expressed through perfect economy — five words that are simultaneously a theological observation and a devastating insult. The casualness of the delivery makes it land harder. Very high quotability.

jokes.text: Only so that they'll disapprove of us... And after Mom's second vodka, and after Buster's second juice box, they're gonna forbid Ann from even seeing my son.
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks why Michael invited the Veals
jokes.punchline: Michael outlines his Machiavellian plan, which includes 'Buster's second juice box' as a parallel to Lucille's alcoholism — treating Buster's childlike juice consumption as equally damning
jokes.explanation: The parallel structure of 'Mom's second vodka / Buster's second juice box' is beautifully calibrated. The joke is that Buster's childish behavior (drinking juice boxes) is treated as equally embarrassing as alcoholism. It also reveals how Michael has internalized the family's dysfunction as a tactical resource.

jokes.text: You'll understand when Maeby starts getting proposals. / [Narrator] Actually, Maeby had given two that day.
jokes.setup: Michael says Lindsay will understand his worry about proposals when Maeby starts getting them
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Maeby has already given two proposals that day — reversing the expected dynamic (Maeby as the proposer, not the recipient)
jokes.explanation: The narrator's cut-to is a classic AD structure — a character says something and the narrator immediately undermines/reverses it. The specific reversal (Maeby gives proposals, not receives them) is surprising and perfectly in keeping with her character. The economy of the joke is excellent.

jokes.text: I can't read what you wrote on this buck slip. You've got the handwriting of a 15-year-old. / Fifteen-year-old. Marry me!
jokes.setup: Maeby's studio boss notices her handwriting
jokes.punchline: He mentions '15-year-old,' which triggers Maeby's deflection reflex — she immediately proposes to him to prevent him from focusing on her age
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The mechanism is ingenious: any mention of a specific age triggers Maeby's panic proposal reflex. The boss isn't even accusing her of anything — he's commenting on handwriting — but '15-year-old' is enough. The Pavlovian quality of the response is hilarious and perfectly characterizes her precarious situation. 'Marry me!' as a deflection tactic is absurdist and brilliant.

jokes.text: Boy, you get a lot of mail from that movie studio. / I write a lot of letters to movie stars. / You seem a little old for that. / Babysit me.
jokes.setup: Maeby is trying to maintain her cover at her grandmother's building — she told the guard she's younger than she is
jokes.punchline: The guard questions whether she's old enough to be writing fan letters; Maeby's cover response is 'Babysit me' — she's performing childlike behavior to seem younger
jokes.explanation: Good joke in the Maeby-con sequence. 'Babysit me' as her improvised response to seeming too old is both a funny non-sequitur and a logical (if absurd) attempt to perform youth. Works well.

jokes.text: That place seems so old for you. / I'll send Dr. Epstein your compliments. / And may I add, marry me.
jokes.setup: The guard continues to suspect Maeby might be older than she claims
jokes.punchline: Maeby again fires off 'marry me' in response to age-adjacent suspicion — the second iteration of the reflex joke
jokes.explanation: Second iteration of the 'Marry me' deflection running gag. Slightly less surprising than the first, but the 'And may I add' formality of it is a good touch — she's making the panic proposal sound considered and polite.

jokes.text: Are you sure this isn't her sister? / What a lovely thing to say. / That's an awful thing to say, Mom. Please.
jokes.setup: Michael introduces his mother to Ann's mother, Mrs. Veal
jokes.punchline: Lucille deploys the 'could be sisters' compliment on Mrs. Veal — the same setup from joke #32 that Michael refused to use — and it lands perfectly with Mrs. Veal, while Michael is horrified
jokes.explanation: A masterful callback. Michael explicitly refused to make this comparison earlier because it would imply Ann is as attractive as her mother (she isn't). Now Lucille says it naturally and sincerely — not realizing it's an insult to Ann. Mrs. Veal is thrilled. Michael's 'That's an awful thing to say' is a perfect button. The joke rewards memory of the earlier scene and lands even harder for it. One of the episode's structural highlights.

jokes.text: I'm so eager to meet your husband. / Yeah. Dad escaped from prison. Did you not know that?
jokes.setup: Mrs. Veal politely expresses eagerness to meet Michael's husband (father)
jokes.punchline: Michael matter-of-factly informs her that his father escaped from prison, treating it as ordinary party guest information
jokes.explanation: Michael's casual disclosure of federal crime to a pastor's wife — 'Dad escaped from prison. Did you not know that?' — is both characteristically AD (normalizing chaos) and specifically on-brand for Michael's slightly passive-aggressive plan to horrify the Veals. The 'Did you not know that?' implies this should be common knowledge. Great deadpan.

jokes.text: Is that Mr. Bluth there? / He's got us, Dad. Come on out. / He's talking to you, you idiot. Cover. / I ain't your daddy!
jokes.setup: Michael tries to smuggle his fugitive father in a car; a police officer approaches
jokes.punchline: George Sr. tells Franklin to cover; Franklin responds 'I ain't your daddy!' — both undercutting the cover story and being a meta-joke about their relationship
jokes.explanation: Excellent Franklin joke. George Sr. commands Franklin to 'cover,' and Franklin's response ('I ain't your daddy!') is both a literal statement (he's a puppet) and a darkly comedic refusal of the father role. The puppet actively refuses to cooperate with the man who controls it. The meta-layer — a man arguing with his own hand about paternity — is very funny.

jokes.text: I'm just messin' with you. Hey, Franklin. How you doin', little man?
jokes.setup: The police officer is told about Franklin (the puppet)
jokes.punchline: The officer immediately greets Franklin warmly and personally — treating the puppet as a real person
jokes.explanation: A nice payoff to the 'everyone loves Franklin' running gag — even a cop on duty switches to enthusiastic Franklin fan mode. Validates Gob's delusion humorously. The 'How you doin', little man?' is warmly funny.

jokes.text: No. Cover her eyes and take her to the limo. / No. Bring her in here, knock her out with this ether, and you stuff her into this garment bag. / Anyone sees, you do the same. Oh, hi, Tobias.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Gob are plotting how to get Lucille to the church for vow renewal
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s plan escalates to 'knock her out with ether and stuff her into a garment bag' — and then cheerfully greets Tobias mid-criminal conspiracy, as if this is a normal conversation
jokes.explanation: The escalation to ether-kidnapping is very dark and funny. The 'Anyone sees, you do the same' is chilling in its casualness. Then the immediate pivot to 'Oh, hi, Tobias' — treating the encounter with complete social normalcy after discussing kidnapping — is a classic AD beat. Character-specific for George Sr., who treats crimes as logistics.

jokes.text: Whenever I get a wee bit scared I hum a little tune / Hum diddle diddly / Hum di-do
jokes.setup: Tobias has apparently stumbled into George Sr. and Gob's kidnapping planning session
jokes.punchline: Tobias (still in nanny mode?) responds to the terrifying situation by cheerfully humming a nursery rhyme, completely dissociated from the danger
jokes.explanation: Tobias's coping mechanism (humming) in the face of criminality is on-brand and funny. The transition from ether-kidnapping planning to nursery rhyme is a jarring tonal shift that works comedically. Not the strongest Tobias joke but contributes to the scene rhythm.

jokes.text: You put a handkerchief over her mouth for a couple of seconds. No more than two though. 'Cause you could kill her. / I don't have a handkerchief. / Fine. Put the ether on the puppet's lips. Have the puppet kiss her.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains the ether kidnapping logistics
jokes.punchline: When Gob doesn't have a handkerchief, George Sr. immediately pivots to 'have the puppet kiss her' — weaponizing Franklin as a sedation device
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The pivot from 'handkerchief' to 'puppet kiss' is both logistically absurd and darkly hilarious. George Sr. treats 'drugging someone via puppet smooch' as a perfectly reasonable improvisation. The 'No more than two though. 'Cause you could kill her' safety caveat makes it even funnier — he's being genuinely helpful about the murder risk. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I ain't kissin' that old bitch. / That's my wife, you bastard! / Dad, that's my wrist! / [Groaning] Hey, man! That's his neck!
jokes.setup: George Sr. tells Gob to have Franklin kiss Lucille with the ether
jokes.punchline: Franklin refuses and insults Lucille; George Sr. attacks Franklin/Gob; Gob cries out that George Sr. is hurting his wrist (because Franklin is ON his wrist); a third party yells about someone's neck
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect comedic sequence. Franklin insults Lucille; George Sr. defends her honor by attacking the puppet; Gob screams that 'Dad, that's my wrist!' — because you can't attack Franklin without attacking Gob. The whole sequence crystallizes the Franklin running gag's core absurdity: the puppet is both his own being and Gob's body part. 'Hey, man! That's his neck!' from an unidentified bystander adds beautiful chaos. One of the all-time great AD physical comedy beats.

jokes.text: Where's your pal Franklin? / Throat's a little sore. And wrist.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Veal asks about Franklin after the violence
jokes.punchline: Gob says Franklin's throat is sore — acknowledging the beating — but then adds 'and wrist,' revealing that his own body was the collateral damage
jokes.explanation: Perfect button to the beating sequence. Gob answers on behalf of Franklin ('throat's a little sore'), maintaining the fiction that Franklin is separate from him, but then adds 'and wrist' — which can only be Gob's injury. The two-word addition destroys the fourth wall of the puppet relationship in the funniest possible way. Highly quotable. Exceptional economy.

jokes.text: I was thinking of giving her, like, a pre-engagement — / Ring? / Wow. That catches me off guard, George Michael. I'm not sure about that. / I think it's gonna backfire on you... And you can't. It's very sacred. / Sacred. / Yeah. That's it.
jokes.setup: George Michael approaches Michael at the party to ask about the pre-engagement ring; Mrs. Veal is present
jokes.punchline: Michael, trying to secretly discourage George Michael, suddenly finds his words enthusiastically echoed by Mrs. Veal ('Sacred. Yeah. That's it') — making it seem like his advice is religiously endorsed, which works against his own plan
jokes.explanation: A good layered joke about Michael's plan being inadvertently reinforced by the very person he wants to alienate. The irony is that his anti-engagement advice sounds religious, which Mrs. Veal loves. The 'Sacred. Yeah. That's it.' call-and-response between Michael and Mrs. Veal is a nice comedic rhythm.

jokes.text: Maybe I should ask if she wants to get pre-pre-engaged. Add another—
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Michael the pre-engagement seems like taking marriage lightly
jokes.punchline: George Michael, trying to be more serious, accidentally proposes adding ANOTHER layer of pre-engagement — making it more, not less, trivial
jokes.explanation: George Michael's solution to the problem makes things worse in the exact opposite direction he intended. The 'pre-pre-engaged' is a nice escalation — and the incomplete sentence ('Add another—') implies he might keep going. Well-observed character moment.

jokes.text: The mere fact that you're laughing about marriage means that you're not ready, okay?
jokes.setup: George Michael starts laughing at the pre-pre-engagement idea
jokes.punchline: Michael, who has been scheming the entire episode, solemnly lectures his son about taking marriage seriously
jokes.explanation: The irony of Michael being the voice of marital seriousness — given that his plan is entirely to manipulate a religious family into disapproving of his son's relationship — is functional but not highlighted aggressively enough to land as a big joke. Competent character comedy.

jokes.text: What's so important that you're dragging me away from this miserable party?
jokes.setup: Michael calls Lucille to the kitchen
jokes.punchline: Lucille refers to her own anniversary party as 'this miserable party' — she hates her own celebration
jokes.explanation: A very Lucille line — she despises the party thrown for her and says so without embarrassment. The economy is excellent. Works because it's so perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Well, I just had an old friend who wanted to tell you how much I miss you! / Oh, who let this little black — [Bleeps] / Hey, Brother.
jokes.setup: Michael brings Lucille to the kitchen, presumably to reveal George Sr.
jokes.punchline: Instead, Franklin pops up; Lucille reacts with a racial slur (bleeped); Franklin calls her 'Brother'
jokes.explanation: The reveal is well-structured — we expect George Sr., we get Franklin. Lucille's bleeped racial reaction and Franklin calling her 'Brother' is a double punchline. Franklin's 'Hey, Brother' might also be a commentary on Lucille's own racial attitudes. Dark but effective.

jokes.text: Who you callin' brother, you hook-ass — / Mother! / [Horn Blows] [Groaning]
jokes.setup: Franklin calls Lucille 'Brother'; Lucille fires back at him
jokes.punchline: Buster interrupts with 'Mother!' to stop the slur, and apparently honks a horn with his hook hand, causing groaning — a chaotic physical comedy beat
jokes.explanation: The hook-hand horn honk is a beautifully absurd moment — Buster uses his hook as an impromptu noise-maker. The 'Mother!' interruption is both a parody of the censoring instinct and a perfectly Buster thing to do. High rewatch bonus for the physical gag.

jokes.text: What the hell kind of puppet does Buster have on his hand?
jokes.setup: After the Franklin confrontation, Buster appears to have done something with his hook hand
jokes.punchline: Lucille asks what puppet Buster has — she apparently mistakes his hook hand for a puppet, inverting the situation (Gob has a puppet that seems real; Buster has a real prosthetic that seems like a puppet)
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The inversion of the Gob/Franklin dynamic is brilliant: Gob has a fake puppet that everyone treats as real; Buster has a real prosthetic that his mother mistakes for a puppet. The fact that Lucille — who presumably knows her son lost his hand — asks this question is also a character joke about her self-absorption. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: God, Franklin. Your breath — Oh, God.
jokes.setup: After all the commotion, Gob interacts with Franklin
jokes.punchline: Gob complains about Franklin's breath — a puppet's breath — as if it's a real and offensive problem
jokes.explanation: Another beat of the Franklin-as-autonomous-being joke. Gob is bothered by his puppet's breath, which is impossible but fully believed by Gob. Short and effective.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. I'm not gonna do it now. / Because Ann's mom doesn't approve of our family? Come on. / It's just some stupid plan your father was trying. / Plan? What plan?
jokes.setup: George Michael tells Ann he won't propose now
jokes.punchline: Ann correctly identifies a plan; George Michael accidentally confirms it exists and then panics
jokes.explanation: A functional joke about George Michael accidentally exposing Michael's manipulation. The rhythm is good but it's more of a plot beat than a pure comedy moment. Competent.

jokes.text: Mrs. Veal admits to Michael she agrees the kids are too young — 'perhaps there's someone else they're meant to meet' — then immediately starts flirting: 'Take me. Take me to your secular world.'
jokes.setup: Mrs. Veal seems to be agreeing with Michael's concerns about the young couple
jokes.punchline: She pivots to propositioning Michael — her concern about her daughter's relationship is actually a cover for pursuing Michael herself
jokes.explanation: 'Take me to your secular world' is a brilliant line — it simultaneously parodies religious language (as if secularism is an exotic realm to be explored), foreshadows the complete inversion of the Veal family's values, and is a hilariously forward proposition. The structural joke is perfect: Michael invited the Veals to disapprove of his family; instead, the devout mother is seduced by it.

jokes.text: Perhaps there's someone else they're meant to meet. / Well, let's just say that in the secular world, one finds oneself with their fair share of temptations. Not that anyone would ever act — / [Mumbling] / Take me. / Take me to your secular world.
jokes.setup: Michael and Mrs. Veal agree the kids might be too young; Mrs. Veal begins to muse about temptation
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Veal's 'not that anyone would ever act' trails off as she immediately acts on her temptation — 'Take me. Take me to your secular world.'
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic structure. Mrs. Veal builds her own punchline: she literally says 'not that anyone would ever act' and then immediately acts. The speed of the collapse from pious restraint to 'Take me to your secular world' is the joke. 'Secular world' as a euphemism for lust/sin is also brilliant — she's using religious vocabulary to describe abandoning religion. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Take it. Take the sacred ring. / Not the snake. That doesn't come off.
jokes.setup: Ann and George Michael are getting pre-engaged on impulse
jokes.punchline: Ann clarifies that the snake ring doesn't come off — she has a snake permanently(?) on her finger that George Michael apparently can't use as a pre-engagement ring
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual joke. Ann's 'sacred ring' moment is interrupted by the revelation that she also has a snake ring that is apparently non-removable. The specificity of 'that doesn't come off' implies it's physically fused to her finger, which is both bizarre and perfectly characterizes Ann's extreme religiosity (abstinence ring + permanent snake = specific pious weirdness). Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: [Narrator] Michael had just been kissed by the mother of his son's girlfriend.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Veal makes her move on Michael
jokes.punchline: The narrator describes what just happened with clinical flatness: 'Michael had just been kissed by the mother of his son's girlfriend' — as if cataloguing a particularly bureaucratic violation
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan summary function is at its best here. The relational complexity ('the mother of his son's girlfriend') is parsed out with legal precision, which makes the absurdity land harder. A one-sentence joke that recontextualizes everything.

jokes.text: [Narrator] Michael had just been kissed by the mother of his son's girlfriend.
jokes.setup: Michael and Mrs. Veal have just kissed on the balcony
jokes.punchline: Narrator summarizes the situation with clinical, genealogical precision — 'the mother of his son's girlfriend' — which makes it simultaneously more absurd and more uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator deadpan — the overly precise relational description ('mother of his son's girlfriend') makes the situation funnier by being so clinical about something so awkward.

jokes.text: I can't believe we're making love. / What are you talking about? We're not making love. / I want to please you secularly.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to stop Mrs. Veal from pursuing him
jokes.punchline: 'I want to please you secularly' — Mrs. Veal uses a religious/secular distinction to describe sexual pursuit, treating 'secular' as a mode of physical intimacy
jokes.explanation: 'I want to please you secularly' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode. It perfectly encapsulates Mrs. Veal's character: she frames physical desire through religious vocabulary, making 'secularly' a euphemism for carnally. The logic is perfect — she's a pastor's wife, so even her abandon is expressed in theological terms. Highly original.

jokes.text: But you kept telling me how beautiful I was. / I was surprised because of Ann. / Well — Why because of Ann? / Because of an old wives' tale.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Veal says Michael told her she was beautiful; Michael says he was surprised
jokes.punchline: Michael explains his surprise with 'because of an old wives' tale' — which is both a joke about Mrs. Veal's age (she's like an 'old wife') and a reference to some folk wisdom about unattractive children having beautiful mothers (or vice versa)
jokes.explanation: The 'old wives' tale' line works on multiple levels: it implies Mrs. Veal is the old wife in question, it suggests Michael expected Ann's mother to look like Ann (plain), and it's a gentle continuation of the Ann-is-unattractive running joke. Good misdirection and density of meaning.

jokes.text: Jolly news, governor. Young master's proposing. / George Michael? I told him not to. He said he wouldn't. / Well, this was before he saw you pounding that sweet piece of veal.
jokes.setup: Tobias (as Mrs. Featherbottom) delivers news about George Michael's proposal
jokes.punchline: 'Before he saw you pounding that sweet piece of veal' — Tobias uses 'veal' as both a reference to the Veal family and as a food/sexual metaphor for making out with Mrs. Veal
jokes.explanation: Outstanding wordplay. 'Pounding that sweet piece of veal' works simultaneously as: (1) a crude sexual euphemism for making out, (2) a literal reference to the Veal family name, and (3) a food metaphor (pounding meat). Three meanings stacked perfectly in one phrase. This is exactly the kind of layered wordplay AD is famous for. Highly quotable, high rewatch value.

jokes.text: Oh, he left with Lady Lindsay — / Tobias, where are they? / It is me. It's Tobias.
jokes.setup: Michael asks where Ann and George Michael went
jokes.punchline: Tobias breaks character as Mrs. Featherbottom mid-sentence, apparently tired of the ruse — 'It is me. It's Tobias.'
jokes.explanation: The collapse of Tobias's nanny persona under pressure is a recurring AD beat. Functional comedy — the disguise falls apart at an inopportune moment, and Tobias's exhausted 'It is me. It's Tobias' has a defeated quality that's charming.

jokes.text: We're tryin' to figure that out. Apparently Pop-Pop kidnapped Gangy, and I have no idea where George Michael is — or Lindsay. / I just saw them drive off. They're gonna go look for Bland.
jokes.setup: The family tries to sort out the chaos at the party
jokes.punchline: Tobias refers to Ann as 'Bland' — a portmanteau of her name and a frank character assessment
jokes.explanation: 'Bland' is one of the great running Ann jokes in the series. Tobias accidentally/incidentally gives Ann a nickname that perfectly characterizes her: a blend of her name and a brutal character assessment. Exceptional economy — one word does tremendous work. High quotability.

jokes.text: Who's the hottie? / This is Ann's mother. / Her?
jokes.setup: Maeby encounters the beautiful Mrs. Veal at the party
jokes.punchline: Michael introduces Mrs. Veal as Ann's mother; Maeby says 'Her?' — the same disbelieving 'Her?' used about Ann, but now about her surprisingly attractive mother
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect callback. The 'Her?' gag has been used all episode to express disbelief that Ann is desirable. Now it's repurposed in reverse — used to express disbelief that Ann's mother could be so attractive. The same two-word question carries the opposite meaning in the new context. This is the 'Her?' gag operating at peak sophistication. Exceptional structure and rewatch value.

jokes.text: Does she look old enough to play Topher Grace's mom?
jokes.setup: Maeby has just reacted to Mrs. Veal's attractiveness
jokes.punchline: Maeby instantly evaluates Mrs. Veal through the lens of her studio job — assessing casting suitability
jokes.explanation: Maeby's dual identity (teenager / studio executive) is perfectly expressed: she evaluates a person's attractiveness in casting terms. 'Topher Grace's mom' is a very specific early-2000s celebrity reference that works as a real age marker. Strong character integration — only Maeby thinks this way.

jokes.text: I had this shipped over from 'Blackstool.' It's what I used to drive the Roger Moores about in.
jokes.setup: Tobias reveals a car he rented to maintain his Mrs. Featherbottom ruse
jokes.punchline: 'Blackstool' is a made-up British-sounding town name that sounds like a vulgar compound word; 'drive the Roger Moores about' implies a celebrity connection that is probably fictional
jokes.explanation: The wordplay on 'Blackstool' is a background joke — Tobias says it casually as if it's a real place. Rewatch bonus for catching it. 'Roger Moores' (plural) is a nice additional absurdity. Tobias's unearned name-dropping is characteristically delusional.

jokes.text: [Narrator] And Lucille awoke, half in the bag. / What am I doing in a Nordstrom bag?
jokes.setup: Lucille has been kidnapped in a garment bag (as planned) to be taken to the vow renewal
jokes.punchline: The narrator says she's 'half in the bag' (drunk), and Lucille literally wakes up inside a Nordstrom bag — the idiom and the literal condition simultaneously true
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode, possibly the series. 'Half in the bag' is a perfect idiom because it simultaneously means drunk AND physically inside a shopping bag. Lucille is both. The Nordstrom brand specificity is perfect — she's so Lucille that even when kidnapped she's in a luxury department store bag. The convergence of her alcoholism, the garment bag plan, and the pun is a miracle of comedic density. The callback to the ether plan makes it even richer.

jokes.text: But first — / Uh, Dad — / we make love. / The partition doesn't go all the way up, remember?
jokes.setup: George Sr. is about to consummate his reunion with Lucille in the limo
jokes.punchline: Michael, also in the car, points out the partition doesn't fully close — he's trapped listening to his parents
jokes.explanation: The cringe element is strong — Michael is about to be a captive audience to his parents' intimacy. 'The partition doesn't go all the way up, remember?' is a great line because it implies this has happened before. The trapped-child-with-inappropriate-parents dynamic is very AD.

jokes.text: Seriously, Dad. The radio doesn't work either.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is still trying to be with Lucille in the limo; Michael has warned him about the partition
jokes.punchline: Michael adds the radio doesn't work either — meaning he has no way to drown out whatever is about to happen
jokes.explanation: A tight escalation of the previous joke. The addition of 'the radio doesn't work either' confirms Michael has fully assessed his situation and is completely helpless. The practical detail makes the cringe more vivid.

jokes.text: [Mrs. Featherbottom] Oh, mercy me! I keep forgetting I'm in the colonies.
jokes.setup: Tobias (as Mrs. Featherbottom) is driving through traffic and has a collision or near-miss
jokes.punchline: 'I keep forgetting I'm in the colonies' — implying he's been driving on the wrong side of the road, because he's maintaining the British nanny persona even while driving
jokes.explanation: Tobias's commitment to the British persona extends to traffic patterns — he apparently drives on the left because Mrs. Featherbottom would. 'The colonies' is a perfectly British condescension. The joke implies a trail of traffic chaos without showing it. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Pastor Veal officiating a vow renewal: '...to love and honor your spirit and flesh. First the flesh. I will caress and tweak. I will nibble and bite. I will blow alternatingly hot and cool.'
jokes.setup: Pastor Veal officiates what appears to be a standard vow renewal ceremony
jokes.punchline: The vows are extremely explicit and physical — 'caress and tweak,' 'nibble and bite,' 'blow alternatingly hot and cool' — delivered in church ritual cadence by a pastor
jokes.explanation: The vow content is both deeply funny and perfectly character-consistent for Pastor Veal — a man whose wife immediately abandons piety for 'secular' temptation. The specificity of 'blow alternatingly hot and cool' is outstanding — it's so precise in its sensuality that it crosses into absurdity. The religious ceremony wrapper makes the explicit content funnier. High rewatch value.

jokes.text: Oh, man. I can't wait to get married.
jokes.setup: George Michael overhears Pastor Veal reading the explicit vow renewal ceremony
jokes.punchline: George Michael interprets the explicit physical content as reasons to get married — he's motivated for all the wrong reasons
jokes.explanation: A great button to the vow scene. George Michael's naïve enthusiasm ('I can't wait to get married') in response to hearing something extremely explicit is both funny and poignant — he's motivated entirely by innocently misunderstanding what he heard as romantic. The reversal of expected teen response (lust vs. romance) is charming.

jokes.text: Will you one day... uh, intend to marry me? / It would be great to hear back on this before the real hard A.P. Stuff kicks in.
jokes.setup: George Michael's pre-engagement proposal to Ann
jokes.punchline: He frames the proposal like a professional deadline: 'It would be great to hear back on this before the real hard A.P. Stuff kicks in' — treating the most romantic moment of his young life like a scheduling request
jokes.explanation: One of the best George Michael jokes in the series. The formal, corporate framing of a romantic proposal — 'I would love to hear back on this before the A.P. stuff kicks in' — is pitch-perfect characterization. He's simultaneously deeply sincere and completely unable to speak the language of romance. The A.P. deadline treating his proposal like a work deliverable is both pathetic and endearing. High quotability, exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: Let's do it. Now. / Let's actually get married. / Right now? / My father's in there. He can do it as soon as he finishes. / 'I'll always be here for you to rest your ankles upon my shoulders.'
jokes.setup: Ann responds to George Michael's pre-proposal by escalating immediately to actual marriage
jokes.punchline: Ann says her father can marry them as soon as he finishes his current ceremony, and we immediately cut back to Pastor Veal reading even more explicit vow content ('rest your ankles upon my shoulders')
jokes.explanation: Superb escalation and callback structure. The scene cuts from Ann's innocent 'as soon as he finishes' to Pastor Veal saying 'I'll always be here for you to rest your ankles upon my shoulders' — which is clearly a sex position. The timing of the intercutting is what makes it land. The irony that Ann is about to marry because of her father's erotic ceremony instructions is also darkly great.

jokes.text: Uh, faster, Pastor.
jokes.setup: Michael has arrived at the chapel and is trying to stop the ceremony
jokes.punchline: George Michael, wanting to beat his father to the altar, urges 'faster, Pastor' — an accidental/intentional rhyme that is both practical instruction and inadvertent wordplay
jokes.explanation: 'Faster, Pastor' is an outstanding piece of economy — two words, perfect rhyme, completely in character (George Michael is panicked and earnest), and it functions as both a direction and an accidental pun. Very quotable. The show doesn't draw attention to the rhyme, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: [Narrator] Oscar charged at George Sr., finally willing to fight for Lucille. But they did think alike... and neither could land a blow.
jokes.setup: Oscar decides to fight George Sr. for Lucille
jokes.punchline: As identical twins, they think alike — so they anticipate each other's every move perfectly and neither can land a punch
jokes.explanation: A brilliant concept joke. The identical twins fighting premise is subverted by the logical consequence: they think exactly alike, so the fight is perfectly stalemate. It's a physical comedy concept that works purely as logic. 'But they did think alike' is a perfect setup for the absurd payoff. The visual of two men throwing simultaneous, identical, ineffective punches must be outstanding.

jokes.text: Dad, do us. We're ready to get married.
jokes.setup: George Michael sees Pastor Veal in the middle of the chaos and asks him to marry them immediately
jokes.punchline: 'Dad, do us' — unintentionally phrased as an extremely ambiguous instruction, especially given the explicit vow content we've just heard
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'do us' in the context of the explicit vow ceremony is unintentional but funny. George Michael's complete obliviousness to how this sounds is perfectly in character. Works as a light cringe moment.

jokes.text: You kissed my wife? / No. She started kissing me. / You really should lock that down.
jokes.setup: Pastor Veal discovers Michael kissed his wife
jokes.punchline: 'You really should lock that down' — Mrs. Veal echoes her own earlier advice (she told Michael the Veals 'got serious around his age' and 'you gotta lock that down'), now applied to her own marital fidelity. It's both ironic (she's the one who kissed Michael) and reveals her character's predatory logic.
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback deployment. Mrs. Veal's earlier advice about locking down relationships comes back in the context of her own infidelity — she's simultaneously advising Michael while being the problem. The irony is tight and the economy is excellent. Strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: You kissed my wife? / No. She started kissing me. / You really should lock that down.
jokes.setup: Pastor Veal confronts Michael about kissing his wife
jokes.punchline: Michael's defense ('she started it') prompts Pastor Veal to echo the exact same pragmatic advice his wife gave earlier: 'You really should lock that down'
jokes.explanation: An outstanding callback. Mrs. Veal said 'you gotta lock that down' about young romance earlier. Now Pastor Veal, furious that his wife kissed another man, uses the exact same phrase — but now it's about controlling his wife. The phrase takes on a completely different and darker meaning in context. This is the kind of layered callback AD does better than any other show.

jokes.text: Put your hands up, or we'll take that as a sign of aggression against us. / They're not up! He's aggressive!
jokes.setup: The narrator says police were delayed when they thought they cornered a kidnapping suspect
jokes.punchline: The police immediately interpret not-raising-hands (when hands can't be raised) as aggression — a satirical comment on police procedure
jokes.explanation: A sharp satirical moment about police overreaction — very much in the AD vein of institutional satire. The twist that the 'suspect' apparently can't raise their hands (likely Buster, given context of hook hand) makes it funnier. 'They're not up! He's aggressive!' is a good line.

jokes.text: Oscar, I don't want her. I don't want either of them. / Oh. Sorry. Where's my brother?
jokes.setup: Oscar and George Sr. are fighting over Lucille in the chapel
jokes.punchline: George Sr. says he doesn't want Lucille or anyone else; Oscar immediately accepts this and asks where his brother is — the love triangle collapses instantly
jokes.explanation: The deflation of the romantic conflict in one line is very funny. All the setup and tension around the love triangle evaporates when George Sr. says he doesn't want anyone, and Oscar's immediate 'Oh. Sorry. Where's my brother?' treats the whole thing as a minor misunderstanding. Very AD in its anti-climax.

jokes.text: Look at all the bobbies. [Chuckles] / Oh, no. My wig seems to have run off. / [Narrator] On George Sr., as it turned out.
jokes.setup: Tobias (as Mrs. Featherbottom) arrives at the chapel, surveys the police
jokes.punchline: Tobias discovers his wig is gone; the narrator reveals it's now on George Sr.'s head
jokes.explanation: A beautifully efficient visual joke. The wig has migrated from Tobias (in disguise) to George Sr. (a fugitive), which simultaneously reveals George Sr. in the most absurd possible way. The narrator's 'On George Sr., as it turned out' is a perfectly timed deadpan. Layers: Tobias's disguise failing, George Sr.'s reveal, the visual of a fugitive in a nanny wig.

jokes.text: Who is this guy? / This guy is my husband.
jokes.setup: The police see a man in a nanny wig (George Sr.) and are confused
jokes.punchline: Lucille identifies the cross-dressed man as her husband with complete composure — no explanation, no embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Lucille's unfazed identification of her cross-dressed fugitive husband is perfectly character-specific. Her dignity in the face of complete absurdity is a reliable comedic engine. The economy of 'This guy is my husband' with no further comment is excellent.

jokes.text: [Mrs. Veal] Terry, I don't know what happened. I fell under the spell of that horrible secular family. Forgive me. / Could've used that horrible family stuff about a half hour ago.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Veal confesses to her husband that the Bluth family corrupted her
jokes.punchline: Michael's aside — 'Could've used that horrible family stuff about a half hour ago' — acknowledges that this is exactly the kind of disapproval he was trying to engineer earlier, but it's now arrived too late to help
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to Michael's original scheme. Michael's goal all episode was to get the Veals to consider his family 'horrible' — and now they do, but it's after all his plans have backfired. The wry 'Could've used that about a half hour ago' is a perfect self-aware button. Strong economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Mrs. Veal: I fell under the spell of that horrible secular family. Forgive me. / [Michael] Could've used that horrible family stuff about a half hour ago.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Veal repents to her husband for kissing Michael; Michael's plan was to use his family to drive off the Veals
jokes.punchline: 'Could've used that horrible family stuff about a half hour ago' — Michael points out that Mrs. Veal's horror at his family arrived too late to stop the chaos it was supposed to cause
jokes.explanation: Good ironic callback — the plan worked but at completely the wrong time. Michael's resigned observation is a perfect straight-man reaction. Good economy.

jokes.text: I hope you notice I didn't run. / You fought for me. / What I meant to say is I — I think I broke my ankle.
jokes.setup: Oscar tells Lucille he didn't run, implying he stayed and fought for her
jokes.punchline: Lucille interprets it romantically; Oscar corrects her — he literally means he couldn't run because he broke his ankle
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection joke. Oscar's statement ('I didn't run') sounds romantic and courageous; the correction ('I think I broke my ankle') deflates it perfectly. The structure is tight and the timing is good. Effective anti-romantic comedy.

jokes.text: I hope you notice I didn't run. / You fought for me. / What I meant to say is I— I think I broke my ankle.
jokes.setup: Oscar declares he fought for Lucille and didn't run — a romantic gesture
jokes.punchline: He immediately reveals he stayed because he physically can't run — 'I think I broke my ankle' — deflating the romantic claim completely
jokes.explanation: Textbook misdirection setup-punchline. 'I hope you notice I didn't run' leads Lucille (and the audience) to think Oscar is declaring bravery and love. 'I think I broke my ankle' is the perfect deflation — he couldn't run. The structure is clean and the payoff is satisfying.

jokes.text: Let's alternate hot and cool on that.
jokes.setup: Oscar reveals he broke his ankle; presumably needs medical attention
jokes.punchline: George Sr. quotes back the exact phrasing from the explicit vow renewal ceremony ('blow alternatingly hot and cool') but applies it to ankle treatment — the erotic language repurposed as medical advice
jokes.explanation: One of the best callbacks in the episode. 'Alternate hot and cool' was an explicitly sexual instruction in Pastor Veal's ceremony. George Sr. deploys it as an ankle treatment with complete seriousness — either he genuinely thinks it's medical advice, or he's making a joke. Either reading is funny. The recontextualization of erotic language as physical therapy is a perfect AD joke. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: You need to get to know each other better... and then you can be comfortable going all the way with it. / Do you mean it? / Yes. This time I'm being honest with you.
jokes.setup: Michael advises George Michael to get to know Ann better before committing
jokes.punchline: 'This time I'm being honest with you' — Michael inadvertently admits that he's been dishonest with his son this entire episode
jokes.explanation: A nice self-aware moment. 'This time I'm being honest' is a great line because it acknowledges Michael's entire episode-long deception. It's both character honesty (rare for any Bluth) and an inadvertent confession. Works as a button to the episode's main plot.

jokes.text: It sounds like your father just gave us permission to start doing it. / You must teach me, George Michael. You must teach me the ways of the secular flesh.
jokes.setup: Michael gives George Michael permission to 'get to know each other better' and 'go all the way' with the relationship
jokes.punchline: Ann misinterprets Michael's metaphorical speech as literal sexual permission, and immediately asks George Michael to teach her 'the ways of the secular flesh' — directly echoing Mrs. Veal's language
jokes.explanation: The episode's best final joke. Ann — the most pious, repressed character in the episode — uses her mother's exact language ('secular flesh') to describe her desire. The callback to Mrs. Veal's 'secular world' seduction is perfect; the apple doesn't fall far from the tree. George Michael's horror is implied. The joke also backfires on Michael's parental advice spectacularly. Outstanding character integration and callback structure. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: [On the next Arrested Development] Buster finds someone else interested in kissing his hook. / Oh, God! / Drug dog's got something. Get him, Bennett!
jokes.setup: Teaser for next episode: 'Buster finds someone else interested in kissing his hook'
jokes.punchline: The 'someone else' is a drug-sniffing dog — the police dog is attracted to Buster's hook for mysterious reasons
jokes.explanation: The teaser callback to Oscar kissing Buster's hook — now a drug dog is equally interested — is a perfectly dark escalation. The implication (what is ON that hook?) is funnier than any explicit answer. The 'Oh, God!' sells the surprise. Strong running-gag callback.

jokes.text: They're not gonna let you in at the country club with that. / I don't want no part of your tight-ass country club, you freak bitch!
jokes.setup: Buster apparently gets a hook replacement; someone comments on country club dress code
jokes.punchline: Franklin (voiced by Gob) delivers an extremely aggressive response that ends the teaser
jokes.explanation: Franklin's 'freak bitch' sign-off is a great button for the episode/teaser. The character maintains complete consistency — Franklin is aggressively oppositional to any social institution. 'Tight-ass country club, you freak bitch' is very Franklin. Good energy to close on.

tmdb_overview: George Sr. tries to find a way to attend an anniversary dinner for Lucille while Michael deliberately offends Ann's devout Christian parents. Tobias goes overboard performing his "Mrs. Featherbottom" persona.
insight: Cringe-heavy episode delivers 54 jokes in 54 minutes, just shy of elite status.
